takeaways: CRE capital markets enter a transitional phase as banks re-enter lending and the 10-Year Treasury stabilizes near 4.00–4.25%; Colliers forecasts a 15–20% jump in sales volume in 2026.

Senior living demand stays strong amid tight supply — active adult Q1 occupancy held at 91.2% and seniors' median household net worth (~$335K) keeps fueling move-ins despite housing-market volatility.
Proptech VC investment hit $1.7B in January 2026 alone (+176% YoY) as agentic AI shifts from experimentation to operational deployment across leasing, procurement, and fraud defense.

items.headline: Active adult demand shrugs off housing-market volatility, Q1 occupancy at 91.2%
items.summary: Active adult operators including Amira, Headwaters Group and Avenue report sustained move-in velocity despite a 70 bps Q1 dip to 91.2% occupancy across 125 markets. With senior median household net worth at ~$335K, demand resilience is blunting fears that housing volatility will choke the pipeline — a tailwind for Lloyd Jones' senior-living portfolio.

items.headline: CRE capital markets enter a transitional phase as liquidity returns
items.summary: Banks are slowly re-entering the lending market, transaction activity is climbing off recent lows, and valuations appear to be stabilizing. Colliers forecasts a 15–20% increase in sales volume in 2026 as institutional and cross-border capital redeploy — a meaningful shift for operators sourcing acquisition debt.

items.headline: 10-Year Treasury holds 4.00–4.25% range, decoupling from Fed cuts
items.summary: Most fixed-rate CRE loans price off the 10-Year, which has held a 4.00–4.25% corridor since mid-2025. Borrowers shouldn't expect commercial mortgage rates to fall in lockstep with any Fed short-rate cuts — pricing decisions and refi underwriting should plan for sticky long-end yields.

items.headline: Proptech VC pours $1.7B into the sector in January 2026, up 176% YoY
items.summary: January 2026 proptech investment reached roughly $1.7B — a 176% jump from January 2025 — after AI-centered proptech grew an annualized 42% in 2025 versus 24% for non-AI peers. Capital concentration around AI tooling signals a widening tech gap between adopters and laggards in operations and asset management.

items.headline: Agentic AI moves from experimentation to running real estate operations
items.summary: ICSC reports agentic AI is now automating procurement, pricing, inventory and event programming for retailers and real estate operators in 2026. For senior-housing operators, the near-term implication is agentic workflows in leasing inquiries, vendor management, and resident services — areas Lloyd Jones can pilot at low risk.

items.headline: 71% of leaders report rising stress; 40% considering quitting
items.summary: DDI's 2026 trends report finds 71% of leaders under increased stress and 40% weighing an exit, against a backdrop of U.S. layoffs hitting a 10-year high. With many organizations entering 2026 lean, leadership continuity and bench depth are emerging risks — particularly for multi-site operators reliant on regional GMs.

items.headline: 89% of employees say wellbeing investment lifts their performance
items.summary: Wellhub's State of Work-Life-Wellness 2026 finds 89% of workers perform better when prioritizing wellbeing, and 91% say wellness spaces help them handle work pressure. For senior-living staffing — where burnout drives turnover — wellness programs are showing measurable ROI on retention and engagement.

items.headline: Assisted-living construction costs climbing in early 2026
items.summary: Costs for new assisted living construction are up early in 2026 while independent living costs hold flat. Combined with limited new supply, the cost squeeze should keep development restrained — extending the existing supply-demand imbalance that's propping up occupancy and pricing power for in-place operators.

items.headline: Senior living's 100%-more-demand wave meets minimal supply
items.summary: Industry analysis points to a roughly doubling of senior-living demand against barely any new supply growth over the past two years. The structural imbalance is the central thesis underpinning current cap-rate compression and gives operators meaningful rate-growth runway through 2026.

items.headline: Senior living enters 2026 'at a critical juncture'
items.summary: McKnight's frames 2026 as a make-or-break year as operators balance robust demographic demand against staffing pressure, construction cost inflation, and capital-stack repositioning. The next 12 months will sort high-performing operators from those structurally underwater on legacy debt.

items.headline: Banks gradually returning to CRE debt as yield curve steepens
items.summary: Alternative lenders stay active in 2026, but banks are expected to re-engage CRE lending — especially as the yield curve steepens — broadening the debt menu beyond debt funds and life cos. More competition on senior debt could improve spreads on refinancings closing in H2 2026.

items.headline: AI is also fueling rental and mortgage fraud — proptech racing to respond
items.summary: FBI logged 12,000+ real-estate fraud complaints in 2025 totaling $275M in losses, with generative AI accelerating fake applications and synthetic identities. Proptech vendors are rolling out AI-based verification, but operators should pressure-test their leasing intake controls now.

items.headline: Smart buildings become the new baseline for competitive leasing
items.summary: Automated HVAC and lighting, air-quality monitoring, and sensor-based predictive maintenance are now table stakes for modern properties. For senior housing, the same stack also supports resident safety monitoring — a dual ROI play on opex savings and clinical risk reduction.

items.headline: AI handles execution, so leadership value moves upstream to judgment
items.summary: Across IMD and SIY Global 2026 trend reports, the consensus is that as AI absorbs analysis and coordination, leaders' edge sits in judgment, strategic agility, and human connection. Investing in those uniquely-human skills is the highest-leverage development priority for the year.

items.headline: Flattening hierarchies risk breaking internal communication
items.summary: Most organizations are expected to have fewer management layers by 2030, but shrinking middle management can backfire — breaking communication, disrupting workflows, and fueling burnout. Right-sizing without gutting the connective tissue is the open question for ops leaders.

items.headline: Personalized wellness goes mainstream with AI analytics and biomarker tests
items.summary: 2026 marks a turning point for personalization, with AI-driven analytics, at-home biomarker tests, and advanced wearables moving beyond steps and sleep. The trend is reshaping resident wellness programming expectations in active-adult and IL communities.

items.headline: Convenience now drives wellness spending — products must slot into routines
items.summary: Professionals in 2026 are spending on wellness products that fit seamlessly into existing routines rather than demanding lifestyle overhauls. Operators offering on-site, low-friction wellness programming should outperform those requiring residents to travel or schedule heavily.

items.headline: Deloitte: tech leaders shift from operators to orchestrators
items.summary: Deloitte's 2026 Global Technology Leadership Study finds CIOs and CTOs being asked to orchestrate cross-functional AI strategy rather than run IT operations. The mandate change has direct implications for how Lloyd Jones structures tech reporting lines and vendor portfolios.

items.headline: Recovery moves from athlete-only to mainstream professional wellness
items.summary: Long work hours and screen time are pushing more professionals into recovery-focused practices — from contrast therapy to red-light and structured downtime. Senior-living amenity programming can lean into the trend with low-cost recovery-room conversions.

items.headline: Wellness spaces double as community hubs in workplaces and residences
items.summary: 91% of workers say gyms, yoga studios, and wellness hubs help them manage pressure, and the same dynamic plays out in senior communities where wellness spaces become primary social anchors. Programming density in these spaces is a measurable retention lever.
